sentences.text: Right.
Well, my mother was always the shaker and doer.
And she went from embassy to embassy, to Lebanon to wherever.
And different countries.
My father, I must say, did not want to come to America.
Because my mother always had said, she can go on the tourist visa on a trip to America and try to find an affidavit.
And she would have found one, because my mother was a very efficient and energetic woman.
But he said no.
He does not want to go to America for some reason.
And well, times were very bad at that time in America.
And he knew.
And these people from Stanley Works said no, they will not give-- they don't employ Jews in America.
And they will send money anywhere in the world to help us.
Because the director from the German firm, which Stanley had written to them and implored them to help us.
He said, they will send money anywhere in the world, but they will not give affidavits and-- or give him a job in-- in-- in Massachusetts, where the factory is.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different tries to get visas, I think, my mother-- they had friends who had already immigrated to South America, I think, to Uruguay or-- I think Uruguay.
And they had written, they can get us a visa if we-- if we send $200 in dollars.
So this director did wire the $200.
But he wired them to Arthur Blachmann.
Well, Arthur Blachmann was sitting in Breslau, and this money could never be paid out.
So, you know, at this time, things didn't move as quickly as today.
So by the time that was written out, it was too late.
So finally, we had this cousin, [NON-ENGLISH], in Cuba.
And she wrote that she can get visas for us if we send the money.
So then they did the right-- they sent the money to her address, and she supposedly got us visas, but only for my parents and me, and my-- not for my grandparents.
So we decided that at this point, we are not-- we cannot help it.
We're going to leave the grandparents back in Germany.
And my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we got passage on the Saint Louis from the Hamburg America line for the 13th of May in 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
But that was from November the until May it took to get all this straightened out.
In the meantime, we had moved to a very small place [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and sold a lot of our belongings and just kept what we were going to take along.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we packed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they called a lift.
They were just great big wooden crates, but heavy-- heavier than crates.
And they were in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] square.
They were like-- like closets today.
So we packed everything in there that we were allowed to take in the meantime.
Before that, we had to bring all the jewelry and all the silver and everything to a certain place to give to the Germans.
And we were allowed two settings a person to take along, that's all, two silver settings.

And while we were packing, an official from the government came to the house to watch that we didn't put anything into this-- into this lift other than household goods and dishes and things like that.
So I have to tell you also that when they bought these tickets on the Saint Louis, the line, the Hamburg America line, asked already for return tickets.
So they had already an inkling that this ship will not be able to land.
Or in any event, they wanted the money for-- for both-- for both ways.
But they were so glad that they got even a passage, that they got this cabin on the ship, that they took what they could, and they paid-- paid that and never even thought much about that.
So it was a terrible scene that I will never forget when we left our grandparents, you know, especially my mother's mother screamed like terrible.
But we left.
And we went to Hamburg.
And we got on the boat.
And I was only 16 at this time, so I didn't take it so terribly you know, hard.
I was with my parents, and it was an ocean voyage, my first ocean voyage.
My mother had made a long party dress for me, and I was learning how to dance no the ship.
And I-- you know, before that, in Germany, I belonged to the [?
Vakloid, ?] I belonged to the Zionist youth group.
And we didn't indulge in any of this.
We had-- I remember, we had a letter in school from a girl that had immigrated to Brazil already.
And she said, you wouldn't believe it.
The children here, they wear lipstick, and they wear nail polish.
I mean, for us, that was so unusual and so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we didn't believe in all that.
We wore heavy shoes, and blouses, and skirts, and we were Zionist pioneers, you know.
And I wanted to go to Israel too on the Youth Aliyah.
But my eyes were very bad, and they wouldn't take me.
So I even had traveled to Berlin by myself to be re-examined, to have my eyes re-examined.
And on the morning that we-- we were already on the train, and my grandfather was at the train, and he brought me the letter that I was finally accepted.
But I-- it was too late, and maybe it was for the best.
I don't know.
But a lot of my girlfriends, and especially from that youth organization, went on Youth Aliyah to-- to Israel.
And still-- some are still there.
Did you-- were both sets of grandparents alive at this time when you were leaving?
Yeah, well, my grandmother, my father's mother, was a window, yeah.
And the other set was still--
Yeah, they were both-- yeah.
Oh, it was terrible.
It was just terrible.
I don't know how we-- because we were very close with the grandparents.
And-- but my mother had always had a vision that she will send for them.
And while we're there, while we're in Cuba, we will get visas for them too, and they will follow.
It was a rationalization, but, you know.
But they had me.
They had a young daughter.
And they probably felt that it was more important for the young people to-- to leave.
I-- today, I never understood quite how they could leave them.
But today as I got older, I, you know, I understand it a little bit better.
It was terrible.
Well, anyhow, we got on the-- on the Saint Louis, and we-- going there was fine.
You know.
We had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a German ship, but it was not like on the Nazi.
There were all-- there were about 1,000 people on this ship, and most of them were Jews.
There were a few non-Jews and a few other passengers, but most of them were refugees.
But the crew was very respectful and very nice.
There was no anti-Semitism or anything.
And like I said, I was 16.
I had my first boyfriend.
And I was very busy with all kinds of activities.
And it was fine-- until we got to Havana.
It took 10 days, at the time, I think.
Two weeks or 10 days.
And when we got into Havana, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the police, the-- the immigration officers came on board.
And they set themselves up in the dining room.
We all filed in and showed our visas and our passports.
And my parents went down to the cabin, and we packed.
And everything seemed to be going all right.
So then that night, they said, well, it's a slight delay.
I think you're going to stay overnight on board ship yet.
And it was hot and humid, and you know, May, and we weren't used to it.
We stayed most of night up on-- on-- on board, on deck.
And we weren't right in the harbor.
We were a little bit out.
You know, the big-- And so they-- every day was something else.
We stayed more or less a week, as I remember, in-- in Havana.
And people were, of course, very desperate.
Because a lot of families were already separated, where the husband has already been there, and the wives were on the ship with children.
And so there were little boats coming from Havana and surrounding the Saint Louis.
And they were screaming, you know, how is this and how is that to each other one, and they-- A couple of men jumped and-- into the ocean.
And I mean, it was a very bad situation.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sent telegrams to President Roosevelt and to the Congress here and to the Jewish communities--
Who's they?
Who's they?
Well, I think they quickly formed a committee.
How us Jews-- a thousand Jews on a ship are going to form a committee.
[CHUCKLES] So they had lawyers on board and-- and, you know, smart.
And they sent telegrams.
And-- and the Jewish agency here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish Appeal, or whatever it was at that time, got very involved.
And they came-- two people came down, flew down from America and had some negotiations with the Cuban president.
And he wanted another $500 per head to let us down.
And that was a lot of money at that time.
But I understand they had rallies here, and they had benefits with Charlie Chaplin and all kinds of things to raise the money.
But they said, no, they're not going to pay $500 a head.
They thought they were smart.
So after about a week, he said, you know what?
Take your Jews out of here, and put them wherever you can.
And we won't let them in under any circumstances.
So these visas apparently were issued by a previous government.
And I think the government had changed in the meantime-- or we weren't quite sure what happened.
And today, it's-- of course you can research that.
But that's what we were told.
What was this like for you, not being able to leave?
Oh, it was terrible.
We didn't know what's going to-- you know, there you are, a thousand people on a ship, and you don't know what-- where you're going to go.
And most of all, there was always the danger of being-- being sent back to Germany.
Well, I think she came also on a ship on-- on one of these little boats, yes.
And we were standing always on the railing.
And-- and they were down.
But it is a pretty distance from the deck down to the ocean.
But we did see her.
She came down.
She came a couple times.
She sent us pineapples up, and-- But after about a week, they said that's it.
We have to leave.
So the ships had started up again.
And we went back.
We came by Florida and Miami.
And I learned now that the committee wanted the-- the captain to make a-- a landing, you know, an illegal landing.
And he was willing, I think.
The captain was very much for us.
He was a very nice man, Captain Schroder.
And-- but it didn't happen.
It was impossible.
The American Coast Guard surrounded us while we were in the waters of Miami.
And it-- it-- it was impossible.
They just wouldn't allow us to do that.
And they watched us all the time.
So now what?
They came-- this committee started again sending telegraphs all over the world.
And the rumors were flying.
We're going to go to Jamaica, or we're going to go here, or we're going to go there.
All the exotic places that nobody wanted to go to.
But they were all rumors.
So finally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think after about 10 days of that, where the situation on the ship was becoming very precarious.
They didn't have enough food.
The food became scarce.
And most of all, the people were so desperate and wary of what's going to happen.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I understand that in Paris, this Jewish committee there made arrangements with England and Holland and Belgium and France to split us up into four groups.
And these four countries were willing to take a fourth of-- of the ship.
And I must add that out of the 1,000 people, about 750 had American affidavits, only their numbers weren't called.
You see, we had-- you had to have a sponsor, and he gave an affidavit.
And then you went to the embassy, and they gave you a number.
And the number, that could take sometimes a year or two, because they had a quota system.
And people were called when their number was up.
But they didn't want to sit in Germany and wait for this number.
They wanted to sit in Cuba where they thought they are free and safe and wait for their number and be called to immigrate to America.
So it was only a very small portion of that ship's population that-- that didn't have affidavits.
And there wouldn't have been any problem, I'm sure, that enough American families would have given affidavits to maybe 250 people.
Anyhow, we-- my parents and I, we were one of the first.
We landed in Antwerp.
And-- we-- we got off the boat in Antwerp, Belgium.
I don't remember that.
I really don't.
It was-- you know, on the going there, it was very grandiose.
And we had big menus, and really, like today on a cruise.
It was a beautiful ship with a big dining room and beautiful living quarters and living rooms where we had even Jewish services, Friday night services, and libraries, and music, and dancing.
And it was like a regular ocean voyage.
You know, we weren't treated any differently.
But that all stopped when we come back.
And I mean, we weren't-- I don't think we were hungry.
But we just got regular, you know, food that we-- they had food, but not on that scale that it was going there.
Yeah, yeah.
I had-- it was my first teenage time, really, that I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] more free than ever, especially going there.
You know, I had-- I had a young man that befriended me and from-- and we were dancing.
And we were playing shuffleboard and things like that.
Yeah, I had friends.
I had a very nice time going there.
No, I don't think we had too many conversations.
Because there was not much to say.
You know, we had to wait and see what's going to happen.
Apart from the rumors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't figure out what's going to happen.
I don't think we talked too much about it.
Oh, very somber.
Very somber.
The older people were very desperate.
And this came at the very end that we're going to be divided into these countries.
But I think the same day as that came out, we were already in Antwerp when the same evening we-- we-- we got off the boat, and we came to Brussels.
Now, it just happened, so happened, that my mother's only brother was in Brussels and-- with his wife and son.
And so it was-- in a way, it was nice for us that we were reunited with him.
But and we were grateful that we landed in Belgium.
We didn't know that's going to be a war.
And we thought, well, we will have to start [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] procedure from there to immigrate somewhere else.
We knew we cannot stay in Belgium.

As a matter of fact, when we got off the boat, we had to sign that this is only temporary, and that we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] promise that after times normalize, that we are going to leave Belgium again.
And that came to haunt us later on when after the war, they wouldn't let us stay.
But we came to Brussels.
And the American joined, more or less took care of us.
We were a couple nights in a hotel.
And then they looked for housing for us.
Well, [CHUCKLES] housing was-- Belgium, Brussels was a pretty old city, and especially at that time-- so they found terrible housing for us.
And coming from Germany, where we had, you know, not American comfort.
But we had all bathrooms, and bath tops, and kitchens, and everything.
So finally, they found for us one room and a little kitchen for three people.
And the toilet-- no bathroom, I mean, no bathing facility.
But the toilet was halfways between our floor and the floor above and was shared by all these people.
And there were two or three little apartments like that on each floor.
And every drop of water had to come from this bathroom and bought back.
Dirty, you know, whatever, dish water or whatever had to be brought up the steps to this little bathroom.
It was terrible.
And my father-- my mother took it all in stride.
She-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she didn't flinch.
But my father was very depressed, and he couldn't-- he started to limp, and he smoked a lot.
And he couldn't adjust too well.
But my mother took it all in stride.
She-- of course, we got a little allowance, which was very, very little.
So she started to take in single men for-- for the noon meal.
And she cooked, and they paid little, but enough so we could eat along with them.
And she always found a way.
And then she found, somehow, a place for me to be a nanny, so I get out of this environment.
Because I-- I slept on a little cot in the kitchen.
And my parents slept on the couch in the other room.
And-- and it was just not much.
So she found a place for me with a Jewish family that had a little boy.
And I was supposed to be the nanny.
So I got out of this, and I stayed with them, and more or less was a nanny [CHUCKLES] until-- I didn't see them really all that much, but-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] (WHISPERS) Can you stop it for me?
How long were you with this family?
Well, till the war broke out in May, '40.
But something very strange happened during this time.
I-- on my 16th birthday in Breslau, before we had left, I met a young man who was also 16, just been 16.
I was introduced.
We had-- my mother gave me a little birthday party, because we knew we had to leave.
And he came.
And we hit it off fairly well.
And he came even to the train to say goodbye when I left with my parents.
And his parents were older.
And they didn't want to leave, or didn't have an opportunity to leave.
But his parents-- his mother took him to the Belgian border in Cologne and arranged for him to come to Belgium illegally.
And Belgium accepted illegal immigrants at that time that came directly from Germany.
And he lived with also with his family.
And one day, a friend of this family came in.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they were talking.
And he had been in town-- Werner, my friend, had been in town.
And he must have seen me.
And he said to this friend, you know, something funny happened to me today.
I was in town, and I saw a girl that I knew in Germany.
And I know she is in Cuba, but I would swear that that was her.
And he said, what do you mean in Cuba?
What boat was she on?
And he said, I think it was the Saint Louis.
So he said, yeah, that's her.
She's here.
They-- they came back.
They didn't get entrance into Cuba.
And-- and so somehow, we found each other.
And we were just good friends.
Because he had-- he lived with his family.
And he had a-- there was a young lady, and I think he was his boyfriend, more or less.
But we got together from time to time when he came to see me and my parents.
And we were friends in Brussels.
Then when they're-- on the 10th of May, 1940, I woke up.
I slept in the little [NON-ENGLISH], in the little room under the roof where all the maids had their little rooms.
You know, they weren't living in the apartments.
They had their special-- and that was high up.
It was a high-rise, modern building.
And I hear bombs and fire and anti-aircraft fire.
And I hear bombs.
And I didn't know what to make of it.
So I went down to this-- to my family.
And sure enough, they said, yeah, we're being attacked.
You know, they had a radio on.
We're being attacked by Germany, and we don't know what's going to happen.
So I stayed there two more days.
All of a sudden, I see that this gentleman who was also from Germany originally took to his bed.
So I said, what's happening?
And she said, yeah, go call your parents and tell your father to stay in bed too.
Because they're going to arrest all Germans, regardless whether they're Jewish or not, because the Germans attacked Belgium.
And if he's sick, maybe they will leave them alone.
So I ran to a telephone, and I told my father.
And he said, no, no, no.
I'm not going to do that.
And this is deceiving.
And this is-- if they want me, I'll go.
And you know, he was a very straightforward German man.
So it was all I could do.
And sure enough, they came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to pick up this man.
And they left him, but they left him.
And then a day later, they packed up.
They had a car, and they packed up things that they wanted to take.
And they took off for Portugal and sent me home.
And when I came home, my father was already arrested.
My father was already gone.
And my mother was in this place.
So I stayed with my mother.
And then we heard that-- that in a [NON-ENGLISH], in a military barracks, all these Germans.
And we went there, and we tried to give him something, you know, some toiletry, or shirt, or something.
They were arrested with nothing on their backs.
And the next day, we heard that they were going to be marched to the trains and sent away.
And when I-- we went early to see them again.
And we did see him.
And I saw this young man, Werner Marcus.
I saw him being marched.
He was arrested on the street.
